skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render React and Remotion component code into MP4 videos through the inference.sh CLI. The skill documents input options for TSX code, props, resolution, frame rate, duration, codec, and streaming progress.
skill.summary: Render Remotion TSX components into MP4 videos with configurable output settings.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: Static findings for external commands and network access are mostly true positives because the skill instructs users to install and run the inference.sh CLI. The remote shell installer is a confirmed high-risk supply-chain pattern, but I found no evidence of prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent in SKILL.md.

high_findings.title: Remote Shell Installer
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The quick start instructs users to pipe a network download directly into sh. This can execute changed or compromised remote installer content before the user can inspect it.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is explicit and semantically confirmed by the install instructions. The file later describes checksum verification, but the piped shell path still runs remote code directly.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill permits Bash execution for infsh and provides several infsh app run examples. This is core functionality, but it increases risk because model-assisted sessions can run external commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external command surface is clearly present and intended. The examples use a named CLI rather than arbitrary shell construction, so the risk is elevated but not automatically malicious.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Remote Rendering
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill sends React and Remotion source code, props, and render settings to inference.sh for processing. Users could expose proprietary video logic or sensitive data in props.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network processing behavior is documented as the skill's purpose. It is legitimate for this skill, but the privacy and data exposure risk is real.

medium_findings.title: Additional Package Install Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The related skills section tells users to run npx skills add commands for multiple external packages. This expands the supply-chain surface beyond the audited single skill file.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and relevant to package installation. They may be normal documentation, but they ask users to fetch additional unaudited content.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded Remotion and inference.sh URLs are documentation references. I did not find evidence that these links exfiltrate data by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in a documentation list and do not include encoded local data or secrets. The broader service dependency is covered separately.

low_findings.title: SHA-256 Weak Crypto Alert
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography alert appears to match a checksum reference. SHA-256 checksum verification is not a weak algorithm indicator in this context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 29 explicitly describes SHA-256 checksum verification for downloads. I did not find MD5, SHA-1, or custom cryptographic code in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe Network Download To Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The pattern curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh downloads executable content and immediately runs it. This should require manual review or replacement with a verifiable install path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell behavior is present on one line. This is a well-known high-risk installation pattern even when the source appears legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: Model-Accessible External Command Tool
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash(infsh *) as an allowed tool. This allows command execution through an external CLI when the skill is active.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration is explicit. The scope is limited to infsh commands, so it is not equivalent to unrestricted shell access.

content.value_statement: Teams need a repeatable way to turn React animation code into shareable video files. This skill explains how to render Remotion TSX through inference.sh with controlled duration, size, frame rate, and codec settings.
actual_capabilities: Render a default exported Remotion component into an MP4 video.
Configure width, height, frames per second, duration, and codec.
Pass props into the rendered component for data-driven videos.
Use common Remotion APIs such as AbsoluteFill, Sequence, spring, and interpolate.
Show Python SDK usage for standard and streaming render jobs.
Link to Remotion and inference.sh documentation for further setup.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI or Python SDK and a working account.
Sends component code and props to a remote rendering service.
Only documents imports from Remotion and React.
Does not provide local rendering, asset hosting, or error recovery logic.

use_cases.title: Create Branded Motion Graphics
use_cases.description: Designers can render animated title cards, logo stings, and social videos from reusable React components.

use_cases.title: Automate Data-Driven Videos
use_cases.description: Product teams can pass props into components to generate charts, counters, and status videos at scale.

use_cases.title: Render Prototype Animations
use_cases.description: Developers can test Remotion animation ideas and export MP4 previews without building a full render pipeline.

prompt_templates.title: Render A Simple Intro
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a short text animation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Remotion component for a three-second intro with fading title text. Render it with remotion-render at 1920 by 1080 and 30 FPS.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Social Clip
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a square Remotion video with an animated counter and subtitle. Use props for the title and final number, then render it at 1080 by 1080.

prompt_templates.title: Render A Sequenced Story
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request using multiple timed sections.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Remotion component with three timed Sequence sections. Use spring and interpolate for transitions, then render a four-second MP4.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare A Data-Driven Batch
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a reusable Remotion component that accepts title, subtitle, metric, and theme props. Explain how to render multiple versions through inference.sh.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a three-second welcome animation with fade-in text.
output_examples.output: The assistant provides a Remotion component plan, render settings, and an inference.sh command that produces an MP4 video.

output_examples.input: A user wants a square progress counter video for a product launch.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a component concept with animated percentage text, square resolution, high frame rate, and a short render duration.

output_examples.input: A user needs Python-based progress updates while rendering a longer clip.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes SDK usage that streams render progress and reports the final video URL when output is available.

best_practices: Inspect installer scripts before running them and prefer verified manual installation when possible.
Keep proprietary source code, secrets, and private customer data out of render props.
Start with short durations and smaller resolutions before rendering final production videos.
anti_patterns: Do not paste credentials or private tokens into component props.
Do not run remote install commands without checking the source and checksum.
Do not assume remote render output is private unless the service terms confirm it.

faq.question: What does this skill render?
faq.answer: It renders React and Remotion TSX components into MP4 videos through inference.sh.

faq.question: Does it support Remotion APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents common APIs including useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence, Audio, Video, and Img.

faq.question: Can I pass data into a video?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show props passed into a component for dynamic titles, subtitles, and other values.

faq.question: Does this run locally?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow uses inference.sh services through the CLI or Python SDK.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The quick start includes a remote script piped into sh, and render jobs send code to a third-party service.

faq.question: Is there evidence of malicious intent?
faq.answer: No evidence found in SKILL.md. The main concerns are supply-chain risk, external command use, and remote processing.
